---
title: "Project Report"
output: html_document
date: "2023-12-11"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Descriptive Statistics

```{r}
# Load necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Read the data from CSV file
data <- read.csv("shopping_behavior_updated.csv")


# Calculate summary statistics for specific columns and transpose
summary_stats <- data %>%
  reframe(
    Min = c(min(Age, na.rm = TRUE), min(Purchase.Amount..USD., na.rm = TRUE), 
            min(Review.Rating, na.rm = TRUE), min(Previous.Purchases, na.rm = TRUE)),
    Max = c(max(Age, na.rm = TRUE), max(Purchase.Amount..USD., na.rm = TRUE), 
            max(Review.Rating, na.rm = TRUE), max(Previous.Purchases, na.rm = TRUE)),
    Mean = c(mean(Age, na.rm = TRUE), mean(Purchase.Amount..USD., na.rm = TRUE), 
             mean(Review.Rating, na.rm = TRUE), mean(Previous.Purchases, na.rm = TRUE)),
    SD = c(sd(Age, na.rm = TRUE), sd(Purchase.Amount..USD., na.rm = TRUE), 
           sd(Review.Rating, na.rm = TRUE), sd(Previous.Purchases, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) %>%
  t() %>%
  as.data.frame()

# Set row names
rownames(summary_stats) <- c("Minimum", "Maximum", "Mean", "SD")
colnames(summary_stats) <- c("Age", "Purchase Amount (USD)", "Review Rating", "Previous Purchases")
# Print the summary statistics table
print(summary_stats)

# Visualization examples:

# Histogram for Age Distribution
ggplot(data, aes(x=Age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth=7, fill="blue", color="black") +
  ggtitle("Age Distribution of Customers") +
  xlab("Age") +
  ylab("Frequency")

# Create histogram for Previous Purchases
ggplot(data, aes(x=Previous.Purchases)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth=6, fill="blue", color="black") +
  ggtitle("Histogram of Previous Purchases") +
  xlab("Previous Purchases") +
  ylab("Frequency")

# Bar plot for Gender Distribution
ggplot(data, aes(x=Gender)) +
  geom_bar(fill="cyan", color="black") +
  ggtitle("Gender Distribution of Customers") +
  xlab("Gender") +
  ylab("Count")

# Boxplot for Purchase Amount by Gender
ggplot(data, aes(x=Gender, y=Purchase.Amount..USD.)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill="lightgreen", color="black") +
  ggtitle("Purchase Amount by Gender") +
  xlab("Gender") +
  ylab("Purchase Amount (USD)")

```

## Hypothesis Testing

```{r}
# Create subsets of data based on gender
male_data <- filter(data, Gender == "Male")$Purchase.Amount..USD.
female_data <- filter(data, Gender == "Female")$Purchase.Amount..USD.

# F-test to compare variances
f_test_result <- var.test(male_data, female_data)

# Print the result of the F-test
print(f_test_result)

# Conduct two-sample t-test
# If F-test shows no significant difference in variances, use var.equal = TRUE
t_test_result <- t.test(male_data, female_data, var.equal = (f_test_result$p.value > 0.05))

# Print the result of the t-test
print(t_test_result)

```
```{r}
# Perform ANOVA
anova_result <- aov(Purchase.Amount..USD. ~ Category, data = data)

# Summary of the ANOVA result
summary(anova_result)

```
```{r}
library(tidyr)
# Select only the required columns
selected_data <- data %>%
  select(Purchase.Amount..USD., Age, Category, Gender, Previous.Purchases, Subscription.Status)

# Create dummy variables for Gender and Subscription Status
selected_data <- selected_data %>%
  mutate(Gender = ifelse(Gender == "Female", 1, 0),
         Subscription.Status = ifelse(Subscription.Status == "Yes", 1, 0))

# Creating dummy variables for Category
# Using 'Accessories' as the baseline (not included in the dummy variables)
selected_data$Clothing <- ifelse(selected_data$Category == "Clothing", 1, 0)
selected_data$Footwear <- ifelse(selected_data$Category == "Footwear", 1, 0)
selected_data$Outerwear <- ifelse(selected_data$Category == "Outerwear", 1, 0)

# Remove the original 'Category' column
selected_data$Category <- NULL

# Conduct the regression analysis
regression_result <- lm(Purchase.Amount..USD. ~ ., data = selected_data)

# Display the regression results
summary(regression_result)

```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
